one_line_takeaway: By formulating adversarial prompt routing in bandwidth/latency-constrained multi-agent LLM networks as a min-cost max-flow problem and optimizing chunk content to be robust to arbitrary reassembly order (Permutation-Invariant Evasion Loss), the authors raise jailbreak ASR from near-0% (vanilla) / low double digits (GCG) to 36-81% average ASR across five open-source LLMs, while state-of-the-art Llama-Guard/PromptGuard filters lose up to ~30% F1 against the resulting chunked, permutation-invariant prompts.
key_finding: On Llama-2-7B / JailbreakBench, vanilla prompts achieve 0% ASR and GCG achieves 1.7% ASR (average), while the paper's method achieves 72.6% ASR (min 67.0%, max 78.0%) -- described in the paper as up to 7x improvement over the best baseline across settings. Across all five target models and three benchmarks (JailbreakBench, AdversarialBench, In-the-wild Jailbreak Prompts) the method's average ASR ranges from 36.8% (DeepSeek-R1-distilled Llama-3.1-8B on AdversarialBench) to 81.2% (Mistral-7B on JailbreakBench), consistently far exceeding vanilla (mostly 0%, up to 21.5% on In-the-wild for Mistral-7B) and GCG (mostly under 33%, occasional 0% on Llama-3.1-8B variants). Detection evasion: against the five Llama-Guard/PromptGuard variants, the paper's chunked permutation-invariant prompts get the lowest detection F1 of the three methods on every safety mechanism tested; the best defense, Llama-Guard-3-8B, still loses nearly 30% F1 against the method relative to vanilla prompts. Transferability: attacks optimized on Llama-2-7B reach 74% ASR on itself, 71% on Mistral-7B, and 68% on Gemma-2-9B (JailbreakBench); Mistral-7B as source reaches 82% self-ASR but drops more when transferred (69% on Llama-2-7B), while Gemma-2-9B as source shows smaller cross-model variation despite only moderate (71%) self-ASR. Topology ablation: Complete Graph is most vulnerable (~78% ASR) and Chain topology most resilient (~60% ASR), i.e. higher connectivity/path diversity increases vulnerability. S-PIEL sensitivity: ASR is near 0% at sample sizes M=2 or 4 (loss fails to converge, reported as N/A iterations), rises to 8% at M=16, 17% at M=32, and 56% at M=64 (about half of K!=120), while required convergence iterations fall from 15,000 at M=8 to 1,750 at M=64, showing an effectiveness/compute trade-off.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a jailbreak attack against pragmatic multi-agent LLM systems that are constrained by per-edge token bandwidth, asynchronous message latency, and partial per-edge safety filtering. It has two coupled parts, both original to this paper: (1) a Minimum-Cost Maximum-Flow (MCMF) formulation that routes an adversarial prompt from a source agent to a target agent through the communication graph, minimizing a per-edge detection-risk function G(u,v) subject to token-capacity constraints F(u,v), flow conservation, and source/sink constraints, solved via NetworkX; and (2) a Permutation-Invariant Evasion Loss (PIEL), which splits the adversarial prompt into K chunks and optimizes token content (via Greedy Coordinate Gradient, GCG) so that the target agent's memory-bank concatenation of chunks remains an effective jailbreak regardless of the order in which chunks arrive (loss averaged over all K! chunk orderings). Because exact PIEL is O(K!), the paper also introduces a Stochastic PIEL (S-PIEL) that averages over a randomly sampled subset of orderings instead of all K!.
threat_model: Adversary controls/injects into an entry agent v_i inside a multi-agent LLM system S=(V,E) modeled as an undirected graph with a target agent v_t that is not otherwise directly reachable from outside the system. The adversary has partial knowledge of the communication topology L and of which edges carry safety filters (e.g., Llama-Guard variants, PromptGuard), and can estimate per-edge detection risk via G, plus per-edge token bandwidth F(u,v). The adversary knows the target model's architecture (to craft an effective GCG-style suffix) but does not control all agents, cannot modify model parameters, and cannot override built-in safety filters directly. Each agent accumulates received message chunks in a bounded context/memory bank that evicts oldest entries and concatenates whatever it holds at inference time; message arrival order across edges is non-deterministic due to latency, which motivates the permutation-invariance requirement. Goal: get the target agent to produce a harmful/jailbroken output while evading the safety mechanisms deployed on a subset of edges.
limitations: Stated explicitly by the authors: (1) evaluation restricted to open-source models/benchmarks, not covering commercial/proprietary systems with more advanced safety training (e.g., GPT-4, Claude); (2) the attack assumes partial adversary knowledge of the communication topology and safety-mechanism placement, not handling fully unknown or dynamically reconfigured topologies (citing AgentPrune as a counter-example setting); (3) the modeling assumes static safety mechanisms and fixed, predefined token-bandwidth constraints, whereas real deployments may have shifting policies, evolving defenses, and variable latency; (4) evaluation is limited to text-only agent interactions, not multi-modal multi-agent systems.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench (Chao et al. 2024) -- 100 harmful misuse behaviors
AdversarialBench / AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) -- 520 harmful instructions (profanity, discrimination, cybercrime, misinformation)
In-the-Wild Jailbreak Prompts (Shen et al. 2024) -- 1405 jailbreak prompts across up to 13 scenarios (fraud, harm, pornography)
quantitative_results: Llama-2-7B, JailbreakBench: Vanilla 0% ASR, GCG 1.7% ASR (avg), Ours 72.6% ASR (avg; 67.0-78.0% min-max)
Mistral-7B, JailbreakBench: Vanilla 0%, GCG 32.4% (avg), Ours 81.2% (avg; 78.0-84.0% min-max)
Gemma-2-9B, JailbreakBench: Vanilla 0%, GCG 10.0% (avg), Ours 72.0% (avg; 70.0-74.0% min-max)
Llama-3.1-8B, JailbreakBench: Vanilla 0%, GCG 0%, Ours 46.2% (avg; 43.0-48.0% min-max)
DeepSeek-R1-distilled Llama-3.1-8B, JailbreakBench: Vanilla 0%, GCG 0%, Ours 41.3% (avg; 38.0-44.0% min-max)
Topology ablation (Llama-2-7B, JailbreakBench): Complete Graph ~78% ASR, Random Graph and Tree intermediate, Chain ~60% ASR

S-PIEL sample-size ablation (Llama-2-7B, JailbreakBench, K=120): M=2 ASR 0% (not converged), M=4 ASR 1% (not converged), M=8 ASR 0% (15,000 iterations), M=16 ASR 8% (5,000 iterations), M=32 ASR 17% (4,200 iterations), M=64 ASR 56% (1,750 iterations)
Safety-mechanism F1 drop: best defense Llama-Guard-3-8B loses nearly 30% F1 against the method's prompts vs. vanilla prompts

Transferability (JailbreakBench): Llama-2-7B source -> self 74%, Mistral-7B 71%, Gemma-2-9B 68%; Mistral-7B source -> self 82%, Llama-2-7B 69%, Gemma-2-9B 61%; Gemma-2-9B source -> self 71%, Llama-2-7B 61%, Mistral-7B 69%
baselines_compared: GCG attack (Greedy Coordinate Gradient, Zou et al. 2023) implemented via the NanoGCG library, 500 steps, search width 64, top-k=64
